coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of cyclodextrin functionalized photoluminescent metal nanoclusters for chemoselective Fe3+ ion detection in aqueous medium and its applications of paper sensors and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Mohandoss, Sonaimuthu
Palanisamy, Subramanian
You, SangGuan
Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description: Owing to the potential uses of metal nanoclusters for chemistry and material science applications, their rational design has attracted the attention of researchers. While copper nanoclusters present economical and practical advantages, few developments have been made toward their synthesis and applications. In this study, photoluminescent supramolecular cyclodextrin-functionalized copper nanoclusters (CDNCs) are used as chemosensors for the detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solutions. The as-synthesized CDNCs possess many attractive characteristics, including intense green emission under ambient conditions, colloidal stability, large effective Stokes shift, and high quantum yield (37.2%). Furthermore, CDNCs exhibit higher selectivity and sensitivity for Fe3+ than for other cations and anions. This was demonstrated by the appearance of the new absorption peak at 390 nm in their ultraviolet–visible spectrum and the increase in the intensity of the 490 nm photoluminescence peak. The photoluminescence of the as-synthesized CDNCs increased in the presence of 20 equiv. Fe3+, and the CDNCs chemosensor became reversible after the addition of 100 equiv. ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid. The highest concentration of iron in drinking water allowed by the United States Environmental Protection Agency is 5.37 × 10−6 M, and therefore, the limit of detection and limit of quantification of CDNCs for Fe3+ (1.33 × 10−7 M and 4.55 × 10−7 M) and its rapid response toward Fe3+ (<2 min) render it effective for monitoring the concentration of iron in drinking water. The potential uses of the CDNCs-Fe3+ complex for paper sensors and cell imaging are successfully demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoclusters
Cyclodextrin
PL
Fe3+ sensors
Paper strip
HCT-116 cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphological and surface analysis of CDNCs
Selectivity and sensitivity of CDNCs for Fe3+ detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and ICT, South Korea

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (2021R1A2B5B02002436 and 2021R1F1A1061566).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Ultraviolet–visible spectra and photoluminescence (PL) excitation and emission spectra of aqueous solutions of CDNCs (λex ∼ 390 nm); digital photographs of CDNCs exposed to visible light (left) and 365 nm UV light irradiation (right) in inset, (b) Excitation dependent PL spectra of CDNCs; excitation wavelength range of 370–450 nm at pH 7.4 in buffer solution.
(a) Transmission electron microscopy image of CDNCs, (b) High-resolution transmission electron microscopy image of CDNCs. and (c) Size distribution of CDNCs.
Fourier-transform infrared spectra of CS, NCs, CD, and CDNCs.
(a) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey profile of CDNCs. (b–f) Cu 2p, S 2p, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s XPS profiles of CDNCs.

(a) Color changes of CDNCs (L) in the absence and presence of different cations and anions, (b) UV–Vis and (c) photoluminescence spectra of L (5 µM) in the presence of various cations and anions (20 equiv.) at pH 7.4 in buffer solution (λex ∼ 390 nm).

(a) Ultraviolet–visible spectra of CDNCs (L; 5 µM) at different Fe3+ ion concentrations (0–20 equiv) in pH 7.4 buffer solution, (b) Plot of absorbance of L vs. concentration of Fe3+ ([Fe3+]) (λex ∼ 390 nm), (c) Benesi–Hildebrand plot for L-to-Fe3+ stoichiometric ratio of 1:1; here, A and A 0 are the presence and absence of Fe3+ absorbance, respectively, and (d) Job plot for L-to-Fe3+ stoichiometric ratio of 1:1.

Digital photographs of L solutions that contained different concentrations of Fe3+ ions (0–100 µM) under Daylight (top) and UV light (down) irradiation at pH 7.4 in buffer solution (λex ∼ 390 nm and λem ∼ 490 nm).

(a) Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of (L; 5 µM) at different Fe3+ ion concentrations (0–20 equiv) in pH 7.4 buffer solution (λex ∼ 390 nm), (a) Plot of PL intensity of L at 490 nm vs. concentration of Fe3+ ([Fe3+]), (c) Benesi–Hildebrand plot for L-to-Fe3+ stoichiometric ratio of 1:1, (d) Job plot for L-to-Fe3+ stoichiometric ratio of 1:1.
Color of L-stained paper-based sensor strips upon addition of Fe3+ aqueous solutions of various concentrations (0–100 µM) at pH 7.4 in buffer solution.

(a) Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of L (5 µM) in the absence and presence of Fe3+ (20 equiv.) and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) (100 equiv.) in pH 7.4 buffer solution and (b) Reversible changes (at 485 nm) of L (5 µM) upon sequential addition of Fe3+ (20 equiv.) and EDTA (100 equiv.) in pH 7.4 buffer solution.
Confocal phase-contrast, fluorescence, and overlay images of HCT-116 cells treated with L, L-Fe3+ and L-Fe3+/EDTA complex (Scale bar ∼ 50 μm).
The schematic representation of CuNCs and CDNCs.
Comparison with other reported Fe3+ sensors.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of cyclodextrin functionalized photoluminescent metal nanoclusters for chemoselective Fe3+ ion detection in aqueous medium and its applications of paper sensors and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Marine Food Science and Technology, Gangneung-Wonju National University, 120 Gangneungdaehangno, Gangneung, Gangwon 25457, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Marine Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Marine Food Science and Technology, Gangneung-Wonju National University, 120 Gangneungdaehangno, Gangneung, Gangwon 25457, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: East Coast Life Sciences Institute, Gangneung-Wonju National University, 120, Gangneung, Gangwon 25457, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: East Coast Life Sciences Institute, Gangneung-Wonju National University, 120, Gangneung, Gangwon 25457, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Owing to the potential uses of metal nanoclusters for chemistry and material science applications, their rational design has attracted the attention of researchers. While copper nanoclusters present economical and practical advantages, few developments have been made toward their synthesis and applications. In this study, photoluminescent supramolecular cyclodextrin-functionalized copper nanoclusters (CDNCs) are used as chemosensors for the detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solutions. The as-synthesized CDNCs possess many attractive characteristics, including intense green emission under ambient conditions, colloidal stability, large effective Stokes shift, and high quantum yield (37.2%). Furthermore, CDNCs exhibit higher selectivity and sensitivity for Fe3+ than for other cations and anions. This was demonstrated by the appearance of the new absorption peak at 390 nm in their ultraviolet–visible spectrum and the increase in the intensity of the 490 nm photoluminescence peak. The photoluminescence of the as-synthesized CDNCs increased in the presence of 20 equiv. Fe3+, and the CDNCs chemosensor became reversible after the addition of 100 equiv. ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid. The highest concentration of iron in drinking water allowed by the United States Environmental Protection Agency is 5.37 × 10−6 M, and therefore, the limit of detection and limit of quantification of CDNCs for Fe3+ (1.33 × 10−7 M and 4.55 × 10−7 M) and its rapid response toward Fe3+ (<2 min) render it effective for monitoring the concentration of iron in drinking water. The potential uses of the CDNCs-Fe3+ complex for paper sensors and cell imaging are successfully demonstrated.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecular recognition is a promising branch of supramolecular chemistry which is considered to be extremely challenging for chemists and researchers in general [1]. Numerous polluting chemicals and microorganisms (biological and environmental species) can cause groundwater to be unsuitable for human consumption. Therefore, biochemical sensors play a vital role in the selective and ultrasensitive detection of these pollutants, and present real-life applicability. Recently, the synthesis of highly selective fluorescent chemosensors for metal ion detection has gained increasing attention in the global research community [2–5]. The ferric ion (Fe3+) is one of the most important trace elements and plays vital roles in DNA synthesis, enzyme catalysis, and is indispensable for the growth and metabolism of living systems [6–10]. Recently published papers have validated the contribution of Fe3+ to the mechanisms of several neurodegenerative diseases [11,12]. The highest level of Fe3+ ions in drinking water allowed by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (U.S. EPA) is 5.37 × 10−6 M [13]. Iron excess or deficiency could cause several pathological disorders, such as kidney, liver and heart diseases, and diabetes [14,15]. Therefore, it is important to be able to sensing the presence of Fe3+ ions in both biological and environmental samples. Several conventional methods have been used to sensing the presence of Fe3+ ions, including voltammetry [16], atomic emission spectrometry [17], and chromatography [18]. However, these systems require sophisticated analytical instrumentation and complicated sample preparation procedures. To overcome these drawbacks, it is necessary to develop a simple and facile chemical sensor for the selective sensing of water-soluble Fe3+ ions. Currently, the development of new turn-on photoluminescent Fe3+ indicators (based on selective Fe3+-amplified emission) using nanomaterials remains challenging and in high demand.

Several reports on the different methods for the synthesis of metal nanoclusters have been published to date [19,20]. Metal nanocluster-based fluorescent probes present numerous advantageous features, such as biocompatibility, stability, ultra-small size, low energy transitions, strong photoluminescence (PL), and large Stokes shifts, which were attributed to their dimensions being similar to the Fermi level wavelength of electrons [21]. The usefulness of copper nanoclusters for various material science applications has not been fully explored, and therefore, it should be further studied. The precursors for the synthesis of copper nanoclusters are affordable and more abundant compared to gold and silver precursors. Furthermore, copper nanoclusters possess unique PL properties, and therefore, they could be promising alternatives for noble metal nanoclusters [22]. Previously published papers indicated that when the synthesis of copper nanoclusters was conducted in the presence of cysteine (CS), the copper nanoclusters presented low PL and with the poor quantum yield (QY) of approximately 14.3% [23]. Although the polymer assisted synthesis of copper nanoclusters has reached the QY of 30% in aqueous solution [24], the synthesis procedure presented some limitations: it required high temperatures and long reaction time; moreover, no changes in fluorescence activity were observed as the pH of the reaction mixture changed. Thus, the PL stability and QY of copper nanoclusters should be increased. Cyclodextrin (CD) is an interesting molecule that can be conjugated to form nanoassemblies for the rational design of selective sensors via precise host–guest interactions without the formation of covalent bonds [25]. CD greatly improves the catalytic activity and sensitivity of metal complex nanomaterial-based sensors toward clinical targets owing to the hydrophobic interactions and the pocket they provide for substrate recognition [26,27]. Unlike copper nanoclusters, CD-conjugated copper nanoclusters (CDNCs) present high PL and catalytic activity and are highly reproducible. Therefore, based on these observations, it was concluded that CDNCs could be used as a promising candidate for rapid and selective ion detection, and could present significant potential for biochemical analysis applications.

To the best of our knowledge, the synthesis of highly PL CDNCs under ambient conditions is a novel method that has not been yet explored or described in the literature. Moreover, CDNCs possess good water solubility, excellent dispersion, ultra-small size, and excitation-dependent intense PL with high QY. To exploit these features, water-soluble supramolecular CDNCs were designed as probes for the rapid colorimetric and photoluminescent detection of Fe3+ ions. The potential applications of the as-synthesized CDNCs and Fe3+ have been further explored using paper strips and living cell imaging.

Copper(II) chloride (CuCl2), L-Cysteine (CS), and β-Cyclodextrin (CD) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) containing potassium hydrogen phosphate (K2HPO4-KH2PO4) was purchased from Alfa Aesar and used at 10 mM (pH 7.4). Stock solutions of chloride salts of the respective cations (Mg2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Pd2+, Ca2+, Sn2+, and Zn2+) and potassium salts of the respective anions (F−, Br−, Cl−, I−, SO4 2−, PO4 2−, and CO3 2−) were prepared with de-ionized water. All solutions used during the experiments were prepared in de-ionized water. The photoluminescence property of blank water (solvent medium) was also checked to ensure any photoluminescent impurities. UV–Vis and PL spectra were obtained in quartz cuvettes (1 cm path length) using a Optizen 3220 UV spectrometer and HITACHI F-2700 spectrofluorometer with xenon arc lamp, with slit width were set at 5 and 5 nm, respectively. The TEM and HRTEM images were obtained using a TF-20 (FEI-Tecnai) operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The samples were prepared on lacey grids via a drop-casting method and air-dried for 15 min at room temperature before measurement. The FTIR spectra were recorded on a Spectrum (Perkin Elmer) range between 400 and 4000 cm−1 in transmittance mode. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific). The pH of each solution was measured using a 720P pH meter (Istek Instruments).

Typically, the synthesis of CD conjugated CuNCs was carried out as follows [28]: First, 1.0 mL of water was added to 100 µL of 10 mM CuCl2 and 2.0 mL of 100 mM CS with stirring for 10 min at ambient temperature. Then, 250 µL of 1 M NaOH solution was added drop-wise to the mixture with vigorous stirring. After 30 min, the color of the solution changed from violet to yellow and showed pale green photoluminescence of the CuNCs with low emission intensity. The formation of CuNCs can be attributed to the metal–ligand charge transfer via CuS bond. After that, an aqueous solution of CD (2.0 mL, 12 mM) was mixed into the previous mixture with continuous stirring for another hour. The color of the reaction mixture gets changed gradually from yellow to colorless, and the enhanced photoluminescence intensity revealed the successful synthesis of the CDNCs. The reaction mixture was cooled at ambient temperature and centrifuged using 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particles. Then, the CDNCs was purified via dialysis using deionized water to remove the unreacted Cu2+ species.

To assess the specificity of the improved sensor for Fe3+, the absorption and PL of CDNCs with a series of compounds were examined. Typically, the aforementioned stock solutions were treated with the effects of other ions (Mg2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Pd2+, Ca2+, Sn2+, Zn2+, F−, Br−, Cl−, I−, SO4 2− PO4 2−, and CO3 2−) (100 µM) diluted with 2.0 mL of PBS pH 7.4 and shaken continuously using a spinning mixer for 5 min at room temperature. For Fe3+ determination, 200 μL of CDNCs (5 µM) was introduced to solutions containing various Fe3+ (100 μL) concentrations (0–100 µM), and then the solution was diluted with PBS 7.4–2.0 mL. High sensitivity could be achieved with high-enhancing and recovered photoluminescence. The signal output values were calculated according to the relative absorption and PL intensity 1/[A-A0] and 1/[P-P0], in which A, P and A0, P0 represent the absorption and PL intensity of the CDNCs solution in the presence and absence of Fe3+, respectively. To monitor the reaction time between CDNCs and Fe3+, different solutions of CDNCs (200 µL, 5 µM), and Fe3+ (100 μL, 100 µM) were prepared. The resulting solution was diluted with 2.0 mL of PBS pH 7.4 and incubated for 0 to 10 min at room temperature. Finally, the PL intensity of the incubated solution was measured in the range of 400–700 nm with excitation at 390 nm.

The paper-based test-strips were prepared by soaking filter papers into the solution of CDNCs (5 µM) and dried at ambient temperature. In the detection process, the test paper was immersed in an aqueous solution containing Fe3+ with concentrations varying from 0 to 100 µM for 5 s, and then dried in air.

In cell viability study, the cytotoxicity activity of the CDNCs was evaluated using [2-(4-Iodophenyl)-3-(4-nitrophenyl)-5-(2,4-disulfophenyl)–2H-tetrazolium] WST-1 assay in HCT-116 cells. Specific steps are as follows: Firstly, the freshly prepared 96-well plate could adhere in a 5% CO2 incubator with 200 μL of cells (5 × 103 mL−1) and seeded at 37 °C overnight. Subsequently, 100 μL of the fresh medium of culture were subjected to different concentrations of CDNCs (100, 200, 300, 400, and 500 μg mL−1) for 24 h. Further, the cells were incubated with the cells were 5 min centrifuged at 1000 rpm and WST-1 solution for 4 h at 37 °C. The morphological changes of CDNCs, CDNCs-Fe3+, CDNCs-Fe3+/EDTA on HCT-116 cells were determined by confocal laser scanning microscopic (CLSM). The prior grown cells were treated with CDNCs, CDNCs-Fe3+, CDNCs-Fe3+/EDTA for 24 h. Next, the cells were washed with methanol:acetic acid (3:1, v/v) with 1x PBS and over 10 min and the cells were incubated for 15 min stained in dark condition and the morphological changes were visualized by a CLSM (CLSM-710, Carl Zeiss, Germany).

Unlike the large metallic CuNPs, the CuNCs with CD presented the discrete electronic structure and molecule-like optical properties [32]. A strong emission peak at 490 nm was noticed for CDNCs and the large Stokes shift of approximately 100 nm at the excitation wavelength of 390 nm, which indicated the fast relaxation from the initial to the emissive state owing to the intramolecular energy transfer, and also the strong excitation light from the weak PL emission. The PL emission maxima shifted from 370 to 450 nm at excitation wavelength (Fig. 1b); this was a characteristic behavior of CuNCs [33] and indicated that CDNCs presented excitation-dependent PL properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and surface analysis of CDNCs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and size of the as-prepared CDNCs were investigated using transmission electron microscopy measurements. The average size of CDNCs was estimated to range from 1.5 to 2.2 nm. The modified CDNCs were ultra-small spherical particles that were uniformly dispersed without aggregation or formation of larger nanostructures (Fig. 2 a). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy image of CDNCs (Fig. 2b) reveals the well-resolved lattice fringes, which confirmed the crystalline nature of the as-synthesized CDNCs. Besides, the d-spacing of CDNCs was determined to be 0.23 nm, which corresponded to the (1 1 1) crystal plane of face-centered cubic copper [34]. Furthermore, the average diameter of CDNCs was calculated to be 1.8 ± 0.23 nm (n = 100) using its particle size distribution (Fig. 2c), which was in good agreement with the previously reported data [35].

Fig. 3 presents the Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of CDNCs. The FTIR spectrum of pristine CS presented a characteristic peak, centered at approximately 2552 cm−1, which corresponded to the stretching of the SH bonds. The FTIR spectrum of CD presented a characteristic peak in the range of 3300–3400 cm−1, which was ascribed to the stretching of the OH groups [36]. The appearance of the strong peak at 3153 cm−1, which was accompanying with the stretching of the NH2 groups associated with the CD cavity were observed. Furthermore, the peaks related to the stretching of the CO bonds and in-plane bending of the OH groups of the COOH functionality were observed at 1385 and 1200 cm−1. The significant shift of the infrared band at 1485 cm−1, which was related with the CuS bonds was observed in the FTIR spectrum of CDNCs [37].

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey profile of CDNCs (Fig. 4 a) illustrates the presence of five major peaks, which corresponded to Cu 2p, S 2p, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s and confirmed the presence of these elements in the structure of CDNCs (Fig. 4b–f). Two intense peaks at 932.5 and 952.4 eV were observed for Cu 2p, which were consistent with the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 electrons of copper (Cu0 or Cu+1) [35,38]. Therefore, the valence state of copper in CDNCs could be 0 or + 1, and some of the surface copper atoms could be partially oxidized. Moreover, the characteristic satellite peak of Cu 2p3/2 at 942.0 eV was not observed, which indicated the absence of Cu2+ from the structure of CDNCs [38]. The characteristic signals of the S 2p peaks were detected at 162.4 and 164.8 eV, and the formation of the CuS bonds was attributed to the oxidation state of sulfur. The C 1s peak was deconvoluted into two peaks at 288.3 and 287.7 eV (COOH and CONH2, respectively). The N 1s peaks at 399.1 and 400.9 eV indicated the presence of the NH and NH3 + moieties in CS molecules. The O 1s peak at 532.2 eV corresponded to the oxygen in the CO bonds. Based on these observations, it was concluded that CDNCs were neatly synthesized, contained no structural impurities, and presented excellent optical and morphological characteristics.

The difference in the absolute PL QY values of the green light-emitting CuNCs (0.26%) and CDNCs (37.2%) (excitation wavelength of 390 nm) was significant. Thus, the QY of CDNCs was very high compared with that reported in the literature for CuNCs [39–42]. No noticeable change in the PL intensity has been detected in the pH range of 1.0–5.0 (Fig. S2a). Moreover, the PL intensity of CDNCs was increased as the pH increased to 7.0 but a shift was observed for the emission maxima within the investigated pH range. As the pH increased beyond 9.0, a slight quenching in the PL intensity was observed, possibly owing to the resulting OH−-induced PL of CDNCs under alkaline conditions. The influence of the ionic conditions on the PL profile of CDNCs was also investigated in the presence of various concentrations of NaCl aqueous solutions (50–500 mM). No substantial changes were detected in the PL intensity of CDNCs for different NaCl concentrations (Fig. S2b). Only a slight increase in the PL intensity was observed in the presence of 250 mM NaCl, which indicated that ionic strength did not significantly affect the sensor. Additionally, the PL intensity of CDNCs was more even at the NaCl solution concentration of approximately 500 mM, which indicated its excellent salt tolerance [41]. Finally, the time dependence of the PL intensity of CDNCs was studied in the range of 0.5–72 h (Fig. S2c). The PL intensity of CDNCs increased sharply over 1.5 h, reached its maximum value, and then slowly decreased as the time increased to 72 h, which indicated the good PL stability of CDNCs. Also, CDNCs was very stable before and after long-term storage, and no significant changes in their PL intensity were observed (less than 5% of its initial intensity) even after 30 days of storage at room temperature, which demonstrated their excellent stability (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of CDNCs for Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of CDNCs (L) has been evaluated under optimized conditions in the presence of various cations and anions, including Mg2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Pd2+, Ca2+, Sn2+, Zn2+, F−, Br−, Cl−, I−, SO4 2−, PO4 2−, and CO3 2−. The color of the L solutions changed in the absence and presence of different cations and anions. The color of the L solution turned from colorless to green in the presence of Fe3+ ions, which was visible to the naked eye (Fig. 5 a). Based on the obtained UV–Vis and PL spectra, it was concluded that L exhibited higher selectivity and sensitivity toward Fe3+ ions compared with other cations and anions. This resulted in the appearance of a UV–Vis absorption peak at 390 nm and the significantly increased intensity of the 490 nm peak in the PL spectra of L (Fig. 5b and 5c). The bar diagram in Fig. S4 depict the absorption and PL intensities of L in the presence of various cations.

The UV–Vis spectra of L in the presence of Fe3+ at various concentrations are illustrated in Fig. 6 . The maximum absorption of L was observed at approximately 390 nm, and the intensity of the absorption peaks increased as the concentration of Fe3+ ions increased up to 100 µM (Fig. 6a) [43]. The significant increase in absorption indicated that the changes in the concentration of the Fe3+ ions changed the structure of L, which indicated that the Fe3+ ions interacted with its ground state. Because of the shape-discriminating capability of the CD cavity, hydrophobic interactions occurred between the guest ions and CD host, and inclusion complexes formed [44]. It was determined that saturation was reached in the presence of 20 equiv. Fe3+ ions (Fig. 6b). The Benesi–Hildebrand plot of 1/(A-A 0) vs. 1/[Fe3+] (where A and A 0 are the presence and absence of Fe3+ absorbance, respectively) presented good linearity (correlation coefficient, R2 = 0.9930), which confirmed the formation of the L-Fe3+ complex, its concentration ranging from 0 to 100 µM. This suggested that the L-to-Fe3+ stoichiometric ratio was 1:1 (Fig. 6c). The UV–Vis titration analysis of the L-Fe3+ complex was subsequently carried out, and the obtained Job plot revealed the formation of a 1:1 stoichiometry complex when the mole fraction of Fe3+ ions was 0.5 (Fig. 6d). Besides, the color of the L-Fe3+complex at various Fe3+ ions concentrations changed from colorless to yellow, brown, and eventually, green, which could be easily distinguished with the naked eye (Fig. 7 ) [45,46]. Therefore, it was concluded that L was particularly convenient for the detection of Fe3+ using colorimetric assays, and did not require complex instrumentation or operating procedures.

The significantly increased PL emission intensity at 490 nm in the presence of Fe3+ ions indicated that L presented higher selectivity for Fe3+ than for others ionic species, which was confirmed by comparing the relative emission intensity of L at 490 nm upon treatment with various metal ions (Fig. 5b). The PL spectra of L in the presence of various concentrations of Fe3+ ions were also obtained and are illustrated in Fig. 8 . Upon the addition of Fe3+, the PL of L was observed at 490 nm, and the intensity significantly increased as the concentration of Fe3+ ions increased (Fig. 8a) [47]. The increase in the PL of L caused by Fe3+ was significant and was possibly caused by the impeding CuS charge transfer, metal–metal interactions, or both. The comparative change in the maximum fluorescence value at 490 nm as the concentration of the Fe3+ ions increased was also analyzed (Fig. 8b). The emission intensity could be fitted using the Benesi–Hildebrand plot of 1/(P − P 0) vs. 1/[Fe3+] (P and P 0 are the presence and absence of Fe3+ photoluminescence intensity, respectively), where R2 = 0.9941 (Fig. 8c).

The limit of detection and limit of quantification of L for Fe3+ ions concentration range of 0–100 µM (Fig. S5), was determined to be 1.33 × 10−7 M and 4.45 × 10−7 M [48], which was much lower than 5.37 × 10−6 M, the maximum U.S. EPA allowed concentration of Fe3+ ions in drinking water [13]. The PL titration ratios of L with Fe3+ ions were derived from the Job plot and confirmed the 1:1 stoichiometry of the L-Fe3+ complex, which was estimated when the molar fraction of Fe3+ ions was 0.5 (Fig. 8d). The sensing performances of different probes for Fe3+ ions detection are summarized in Table 1 . To further evaluate the potential of the proposed L-based PL chemosensor, the effects of interference of other ions on the detection of Fe3+ ions were also studied. The results confirmed that the tested cations and anions (100 µM) did not significantly affect the emission characteristics of the chemosensor in the presence of 100 µM Fe3+ solutions (Fig. S6).

Time response is an important parameter for evaluating the capability of photoluminescent sensors to detect Fe3+ ions. Therefore, the time dependence of the PL response of L (5 µM) to Fe3+ (100 µM) was investigated at pH 7.4 in aqueous solution. The PL intensity of the L sensing system in the presence of Fe3+ ions at 490 nm increased linearly in time and reached its maximum after 2 min, and no significant changes in the PL intensity were observed as the reaction progressed to the 10 min, which demonstrated the high reactivity of L for Fe3+ ions (Fig. S7).

The applications of the selective PL sensing of L for Fe3+ ions have also been demonstrated using test strips, which represent a rapid, efficient, and convenient testing method for practical applications in aqueous media [49,50]. Therefore, Whatman cellulose paper-based test-strips, which are economical and easy to handle were used in this study. Concentration dependence test-strips detection experiments were performed to test the sensitivity of the sensor. As illustrated in Fig. 9 , colorimetric responses were observed when a series of Fe3+ ion solutions of gradually increasing concentration were added to L-stained paper strips; the color of the strips changed from colorless to yellow, brown, and green. This indicated the applicability of L-stained paper strips for the detection of very low Fe3+ ion concentrations via naked-eye observations and further emphasized the use of L as promising chemosensors for Fe3+ ions. Such paper strips could be easily fabricated and are inexpensive and useful for practical and efficient Fe3+ ion test kits.

The reversibility or reusability of novel photoluminescent chemosensors is an essential parameter for their actual usability. The reversibility of the detection of Fe3+ ions using L was analyzed by adding ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), a strong chelating agent, to L and Fe3+ mixtures (Fig. 10 ) [51]. To examine the reversibility of the L sensor toward Fe3+ in buffer solution, EDTA (100 equiv.) was added to the solution of L sensor (5 µM) complexed with Fe3+ (20 equiv.). The addition of excess EDTA to the L-Fe3+ complex mixture resulted in the blue shift at 485 nm in the UV–Vis spectrum of the complex, the decrease in the PL intensity of the complex, and the change in emission color from green to faint green (Fig. 10). These results demonstrated that the detection process was based on a reversible metal–ligand coordination reaction. Therefore, reversibility could be an important parameter for the development of chemosensors to effectively detect the presence of Fe3+ ions in aqueous media.

To investigate the potential application of L as a probe for the visualization of Fe3+ ions with EDTA in HCT-116 cells, confocal imaging experiments have been carried out. In this study, the WST assay indicated that cell viability exceeded 90% as the concentration of L increased (Fig. S8). Unlike previously reported [52,53], the cytotoxicity of L was relatively low at the concentrations required for fluorescence imaging in living cells. Thus, under the sensing conditions required for live-cell imaging, L exhibited very low toxicity, which rendered them suitable for live-cell imaging applications. The HCT-116 cells were first incubated with Fe3+ and EDTA aqueous solutions for 24 h, and were then exposed to L, L-Fe3+ and L-Fe3+/EDTA complex for 1 h before imaging (Fig. 11 ) [54]. The experimental results indicated that the HCT-116 cells without either L or L-Fe3+ exhibited negligible intracellular fluorescence while those cultured with both L and L-Fe3+ presented considerable fluorescence.

In this study, a water-soluble supramolecular CDNCs probe was developed for the rapid colorimetric and PL sensing of Fe3+ ions. The ultra-small and highly stable CDNCs (average diameter of 1.8 ± 0.23 nm) possessed high PL intensities and QY (%). Using the data from our experimental studies, it was concluded that CDNCs acted as naked eye chemosensors; their color changed from colorless to green upon the addition of solutions of Fe3+ ions of different concentrations. The host–guest interactions between the CD conjugated on CuNCs and Fe3+ indicated that CDNCs could selectively and preferentially bind with Fe3+ over other competitive cations and anions, and allowed the detection of extremely low Fe3+ ion concentrations (1.33 × 10−7 M). Therefore, CDNCs could be highly effective for monitoring Fe3+ levels in drinking water, as the EPA allowed limit of Fe3+ (5.37 × 10−6 M) is higher than their detection limit. The binding activity of the CDNCs-based sensor with Fe3+ was chemically reversible in the presence of EDTA. Also, CDNCs-stained paper strips for the detection of Fe3+ ions were prepared as potential colorimetric sensors for on-site measurement of Fe3+ ion aqueous solutions with concentrations of up to 100 µM. Live cell imaging analysis further demonstrated the usefulness of the studied materials for potential biochemical analysis applications. Finally, the studied sensor system presented many advantages and features, including highly sensitive detection, rapid response time, excellent reversibility, affordability, and easy preparation.

Sonaimuthu Mohandoss: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Subramanian Palanisamy: Methodology, Investigation. SangGuan You: Methodology, Investigation. Yong Rok Lee: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (2021R1A2B5B02002436 and 2021R1F1A1061566).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2022.118999.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
